topic: Why Mattress Stores Are Always Empty But Never Close

packaging.title: Why Empty Mattress Stores Never Close
altTitles: The Real Reason Mattress Stores Stay Empty
How Mattress Stores Survive With No Customers
packaging.description: A breakdown of the hidden economics of mattress retail: why stores with almost no foot traffic stay profitable, the role of huge markups, low-rent locations, and how a single sale can cover days of overhead.

thumbnail.visualIdea: A lone mattress price tag reading a huge markup, set against the silhouette of an empty showroom

packaging.hookPromise: This mattress store has had zero customers all day, sits in a prime strip mall, and will still be open next year — and it's not money laundering. The real mechanism is hiding in the price tag.
openLoops: How many mattresses does one store actually need to sell to survive?
Why do they cluster four mattress stores on the same block?
Why is the markup on a mattress higher than almost any retail product?
packaging.payoff: A mattress costing the store $300 routinely sells for $1,500–$3,000, so a single sale clears a day of rent and payroll — meaning an 'empty' store selling one bed every couple of days is comfortably profitable, and the emptiness is the business model, not a warning sign.
packaging.whyItWorks: Nearly everyone has driven past a deserted mattress store and quietly wondered how it survives, making the curiosity gap universal and instantly relatable.
